items.why: False friend — a library is 'una biblioteca'; 'librería' looks like 'library' but is where you buy books.

items.q: Which is correct for 'The cinema is next to the park'?
items.correct: El cine está al lado del parque.
items.misconception: El cine hay al lado del parque.
items.random_wrong: El cine es en el parque.
items.why: Students mix up 'hay' (there is/are) with 'está'; locating a specific, known place needs estar.

items.q: How do you say 'next to the cinema'?
items.why: Students forget that de + el always contracts to del; 'de el' is never written in Spanish.

items.q: What does 'En mi barrio hay una piscina' mean?
items.correct: In my neighbourhood there is a swimming pool.
items.misconception: In my neighbourhood there is a beach.
items.random_wrong: In my house there is a pool table.
items.why: It's In my neighbourhood there is a swimming pool.. “In my neighbourhood there is a beach.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: the library. “the bookshop” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Gira a la derecha.. “Gira a la izquierda.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence says 'In my town you can go shopping'?
items.correct: En mi pueblo se puede ir de compras.
items.misconception: En mi pueblo se debe ir de compras.
items.why: It's En mi pueblo se puede ir de compras.. “En mi pueblo se debe ir de compras.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'En mi barrio hay tres tiendas y dos cafés.' How many places is that in total?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 places. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
